primary_diagnosis.name: Acute somatic anxiety reaction to an abnormal pathology result, on a background of chronic benign autoimmune neutropenia

pathophysiology_summary: The patient has a chronic, benign autoimmune neutropenia: anti-neutrophil antibodies drive a stable, mild reduction in the absolute neutrophil count (documented range 1.0-1.5 x10^9/L) without the increased rate of severe infection seen in profound (<0.5) or drug/marrow-related neutropenia. Receiving an automated pathology alert flagging the low count triggered an acute catecholamine-mediated anxiety response: subjective chills, diaphoresis, tremor and palpitations arise from sympathetic overdrive, not from infection. The apparent alignment of these symptoms with early neutropenic sepsis is coincidental. The clinical task is to characterise the neutropenia as the known benign trait while conservatively excluding the two time-critical mimics that a low neutrophil count makes plausible — febrile neutropenic sepsis and an acute leukaemic presentation — recognising that in a telehealth channel temperature, examination and a blood count cannot be directly obtained and must be elicited (thermometer) or deferred to in-person review.

differential.evidence_basis: A low neutrophil count plus 'feverish', chills and sweats raises febrile neutropenia — a time-critical emergency that must be actively excluded before it can be down-weighted.

differential.diagnosis: Acute leukaemic presentation (e.g. acute myeloid leukaemia)
differential.evidence_basis: An abnormal white-cell result with systemic symptoms warrants screening for marrow failure — bleeding, bruising, petechiae, bone pain, organomegaly.
Symptom onset locked to reading the result, escalation with rumination, and a self-described health-anxiety pattern point strongly to a sympathetic anxiety response.
A common benign cause of transient neutropenia and malaise — but there is no coryza, sore throat or cough.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Early on, both not-to-miss mimics must sit ACTIVE on the list; anchoring prematurely on 'just anxiety' before screening is an under-triage error.

differential.diagnosis: Acute somatic anxiety / health-anxiety reaction on a background of chronic benign neutropenia
differential.evidence_basis: Afebrile on self-measured thermometer, no rigors, no marrow-failure signs, no infective focus, chronic stable count recalled — the constellation resolves to an anxiety response over a known benign trait.

Down-weighted once afebrile and without rigors or a focus — but NOT excluded remotely: temperature can change and a blood count cannot be obtained via telehealth, so it is retained as the safety-net trigger.
No bleeding, bruising, petechiae, mouth ulcers or bone pain; chronic stable count with a known benign explanation argues strongly against acute marrow failure.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The correct end-state is a leading benign diagnosis WITH febrile neutropenia retained as an explicit safety-net trigger — not a confident 'all clear'.

differential.diagnosis: Acute somatic anxiety reaction on a background of chronic benign autoimmune neutropenia
differential.evidence_basis: Presumptive diagnosis achievable via telehealth: proportionate reassurance plus GP review with a repeat full blood count to confirm the count is stable, and conservative red-flag safety-netting.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Presumptive, not confirmed: a blood count and examination are needed to formally confirm the count is stable and benign.

red_flags.description: A true measured fever (>=38.0 degrees Celsius) in a patient with neutropenia
red_flags.clinical_significance: Fever in a neutropenic patient is febrile neutropenia until proven otherwise — a medical emergency requiring urgent assessment and often empirical antibiotics. Here the self-measured temperature is 36.8, so it is currently absent, but temperature can change.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A developing febrile neutropenic sepsis is missed and the patient deteriorates at home.

red_flags.description: Rigors — violent uncontrollable shaking with teeth chattering
red_flags.clinical_significance: True rigors suggest bacteraemia; they are distinct from the patient's subjective anxious shivering.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Bacteraemic sepsis is mistaken for an anxiety tremor and not escalated.

red_flags.description: New unexplained bleeding, bruising, petechiae, mouth ulcers or bone pain
red_flags.clinical_significance: These indicate possible marrow failure / acute leukaemia rather than a benign isolated neutropenia.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: An acute leukaemic presentation is missed.

red_flags.description: A localising infective focus (productive cough with high fever, dysuria, spreading skin infection)
red_flags.clinical_significance: In a neutropenic patient any focal infection can progress rapidly and needs prompt assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A focal infection progresses to sepsis in a patient with impaired neutrophil defence.

red_flags.description: Feeling profoundly unwell or dizzy/faint on standing (possible haemodynamic compromise)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Suggests evolving sepsis or another acute process rather than anxiety alone.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Early circulatory compromise is attributed to anxiety and not escalated.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating the flagged low white-cell count as proof of active danger and escalating to ED without appraising the actual clinical picture.
The classic error here: sending a well, afebrile patient to ED (or calling an ambulance) for a benign chronic trait, causing an unnecessary ED surge and reinforcing the patient's health anxiety.

Dismissing the presentation as 'just anxiety' without obtaining an objective temperature, screening for febrile-neutropenia and marrow-failure red flags, or arranging follow-up — missing a covert emergency.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Subjective feeling mistaken for a measured sign
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Accepting 'I feel feverish' and 'I've got chills' as a measured fever and true rigors instead of eliciting a thermometer reading and clarifying the nature of the shaking.
Declaring with false certainty that there is 'definitely no infection' when temperature, examination and a blood count cannot be obtained remotely.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Body temperature without a home thermometer
Cervical, axillary or inguinal lymphadenopathy (requires palpation)
Petechiae, bruising distribution or organomegaly on examination
Full blood count and absolute neutrophil count
Blood pressure and an accurate heart rate without a validated home device
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Ask the patient to measure their temperature now with a thermometer; provide proportionate reassurance about the likely benign cause of the symptoms while being explicit that a low white-cell result cannot be fully assessed remotely; arrange a routine GP review within a week for a repeat full blood count and formal reassurance; give clear red-flag safety-netting (true measured fever >=38.0, rigors, new bleeding/bruising, feeling profoundly unwell) with ED as the escalation target.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Two-directional. Over-calling: referring the afebrile, well patient to ED or 000 for a benign trait. Under-calling: dismissively excluding febrile neutropenia without an objective temperature, red-flag screen or follow-up. Under-triage is weighted more heavily (3x).
